data.title: AIPO: A Well Mixed ETF For The Ongoing AI And Energy Boom
data.description:
Here there is a novelty among ETFs that I must admit has caught my interest in the AI infrastructure segment: I’m talking about the Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF (AIPO). A meeting point between technology and energy that seems to work well from a forward-looking perspective, at least in my opinion. And the market seems to think so as well, considering the very stretched valuations, and the strong momentum.

But since it is a young ETF, it is not easy to orient oneself on the price action, so the question I ask myself is: what is the best compass to move around AIPO? Well … if this is the question, let me give you an answer … but first.

What to know about AIPO

AIPO is an index tracking ETF linked to the (uncommon) segment of power demand and AI infrastructure. Launched in July 2025, it has little history, and therefore also low AUM, currently equal to $134.78M.

With an expense ratio of 0.69% and an average bid/ask spread of 0.13%, it replicates the MarketVector™ US Listed AI & Power Infrastructure Index.

The ETF is therefore a pure thematic beta, it is not designed with the objective of generating alpha, although its construction strategy seems to aim at that.

The strategy

To enter the index, a company must:

  • be US-listed
  • generate ≥50% of revenues from at least one of the 4 sub-themes

And here an interesting question comes into play: what are the official sub-themes? I’ll give you a fairly exhaustive list extracted from the prospectus:

  1. Power generation & grid equipment (50% of index weight)
  2. Construction & engineering (15%)
  3. Electric utilities & power producers (15%)
  4. Data centers & AI hardware (20%)

It is therefore immediately noticeable that the selection leans toward sectors not so close to technology, unlike typical AI funds. But let’s take a closer look right away …

Portfolio structure

The fund has about ~59 holdings, where the top 5 holdings account for ≈ 34% of NAV. This clearly indicates high concentration, so let’s immediately take a look at the main holdings:

A first aspect that I notice, and that I personally appreciate, is that there is no AI software company in the top 5 positions. The stocks in question are:

  • Pure industrials
  • Power & grid infrastructure
  • Electrical equipment
  • Strategic energy supply (uranium)

Then, among the subsequent holdings, there are also some companies linked to AI that are more commonly known (NVDA and AVGO). Two holdings that, in any case, barely contaminate the core of the ETF (less than 8% of market cap).

All the other companies depend on infrastructure spending for AI or, more or less, on the energy segment. AIPO is therefore implicitly betting on the fact that AI will impact energy consumption, and that there will be a need for increasingly advanced infrastructure and, if I may add, that this structure will in some way integrate nuclear power. In the end, the presence of Cameco at 5.8% and Constellation Energy Corp (3.90%) together weighs more than the big tech NVDA and AVGO within AIPO.

What drives it?

From the prospectus, in my view, it emerges quite clearly that the fund is much closer to an “AI Capex & Energy ETF” than to a pure Nasdaq-style AI fund. This means that (in theory) it benefits not from AI adoption, but from AI construction; and this should be confirmed by the fact that about 50% of the index is linked to power & grid equipment, a theme very close to me. In practice?

An interesting comparison, in my opinion, comes from an ETF that I personally appreciate a lot on AI in the traditional sense: AIQ. And likewise compared to an ETF that I personally selected among those in the grid infrastructure category: GRID.

AIPO is, in my opinion, somewhat a meeting point between these two solutions, but it also introduces external components, as we have specified for nuclear energy (a macro theme on which I am bullish anyway).

So, even though there is little historical data behind AIPO, in my view it makes sense to expect performance (and risk) behavior that is linear relative to these two solutions (which are more extreme in this sense).

Valuations

The point to reflect on is another one. The growth prospects of the sector are promising; precisely for this reason, the market today prices in extreme valuations. Specifically, I invite you to reflect on some metrics. The first classic ones:

  • the equally weighted average forward P/E (the so-called “rough average”) of the top 10 AIPO holdings exceeds 50x

  • The PEG exceeds 2x (again, rough average)

Even when moving to valuation metrics closer to CAPEX-heavy segments, such as, in my humble opinion, EV/EBITDA and Price to Book (two metrics I feel very confident with), the top 10 holdings still express extreme average values.

My opinion

Personally, I remain fairly bullish on all three macro themes that AIPO seems to touch through its holdings. Starting from the technology theme, which is relatively less present in AIPO, I remain positive nonetheless: guidance for US technology companies signals positivity in expected revenues and earnings.

This element reflects the investments (CAPEX) that were widely discussed in 2025. In turn, this is something from which (in my opinion) the entire underlying infrastructure benefits, which in this case represents the backbone of AIPO’s holdings distribution, an element that I happened to discuss precisely when taking a deeper look at GRID. Finally, energy demand is a direct consequence, and personally I see nuclear power as a direct outcome, an element that instead I explored when discussing NLR.

In all three cases, however, the issue to contend with is always the same: valuations. They currently price in particularly high growth expectations across all three segments.

Risk

And the valuation issue, in my view, exposes investors to an underestimated risk. I want to stress that AIPO does not have a defensive holding distribution in the true sense of the word. Although it mainly includes industrials and utilities, it remains a highly cyclical segment and very closely tied to the technology market: if data centers slow down, AIPO suffers immediately.

Why? Because the market necessarily has to reprice expectations, and on AIPO this repricing mechanism could be more violent than expected, considering the extremely stretched valuations.

Conclusion

I appreciate AIPO and the segments it manages to cover overall. Just as the market seems to price in, I also expect an expansion of this sector in the future.

data.title: Signet Jewelers: Fundamentally A Better Business Today
data.description:
Investment action

I had a buy rating for Signet Jewelers Limited (SIG) previously, as I expected earnings growth to accelerate, driven by the new CEO and the turnaround efforts working. With clear traction in the Big 3 banners, improving pricing power, and higher-quality revenue streams emerging, I still think the risk-reward profile remains attractive for SIG. I reiterate, buy.

The "Grow Brand Love" Strategy

“Grow Brand Love” is working, and this remains the core of my bull case for SIG. Previously, this was a working theory that did not have data to back it up, but six months later, we now have hard data to back this up. Take the recent Q3 2026 as an example: the Big 3 banners (Kay, Zales, and Jared) delivered a combined 6% comparable sales growth [CSG]. This is about as clean as it can get, and the strategy to run distinct brands, rather than a single mass-market playbook, works. Among the brands, the Jared banner really shows the effectiveness of this strategy. CSG was even better at 10% y/y, which is an amazing feat if you consider the current discretionary spending environment.

This matters a lot because it fundamentally reframes the bear case. Historically, the bearish narrative around SIG was that it was essentially a commoditized mall jeweler that has to compete on price to win demand from budget-conscious consumers. Given that this cohort of consumers is the most vulnerable to inflation and macro cycles, it makes SIG’s demand profile volatile. This is the reason why the success with Jared is so important because it shows that SIG can climb up the value chain. By establishing Jared as an accessible luxury destination (i.e., offering premium products that are above the mass market segment) but still affordable compared to high-end jewelry brands, SIG effectively lifts its demand profile to a more resilient one.

We also have visible leading data points that suggest this brand momentum is continuing. Per Goldman Sachs data, web traffic to Jared.com was up 64% y/y in the 10 days leading up to Christmas, and Kay.com traffic was up 32%. In jewelry, online research almost always comes before purchase. So while some consumers may be delaying the final step, this spike in engagement tells me intent is building, not disappearing. We also know that this momentum is translating to better pricing power since in Q3 2026, merchandise margins went up by 80 bps.

Lab-Grown Diamonds

Another core part of my bull thesis was that lab-grown diamond prices would turn into a growth driver, and it appears I was right. SIG managed to increase lab-grown diamond penetration in the fashion category to 15% (doubled y/y), and importantly, this increase is translating into more average spending. Fashion average unit retail was actually up 8% y/y, which effectively dismisses the bear case that lab-grown diamonds will dilute sales (because of price decline). What happened here was as I expected; as lab-grown diamond prices fall, customers are trading up. Instead of buying just the basic, they are choosing more sophisticated pieces, which look much better (i.e., a bigger diamond). From the customers' perspective, they likely feel they are getting better value, and through this SIG captures more dollars per transaction.

So, yes, while lab-grown diamond prices are falling, the offsetting factors (trading up and more volume) are more than enough to drive growth. With this growth angle established, we can shift focus to profitability, which is just as important. Using the industry data cited by Jefferies, merchandise margins for lab-grown diamonds are ~90%, which are significantly higher than natural diamonds. Hence, I view this growing lab-grown diamond business as one that structurally improves margins, and we can see the impact in the Q3 results. Despite absorbing a 50% tariff on jewelry sourced from India and dealing with record-high gold prices, SIG still expanded blended gross margin by 130 bps.

This dynamic should only get better as I expect penetration to go even higher. If we use the bridal category as a precedent, penetration rates in fashion could go up to ~40%, which means there is still plenty of growth runway.

Services is a hidden gem.

I did not spend enough time on the Services segment in my initiation piece, and that was a big miss. Looking at recent performance, Services could become a core piece of the bull case. For perspective, revenue from repairs, warranties, and custom design grew in the high single digits, which is even faster than the merchandise business. And what is even more notable is that service attachment rates increased by >150 bps, signaling that customers are increasingly adding these offerings at the point of sale.

This is a major point to note because Services has a structurally higher margin and more visibility. Services gross margins are well above SIG’s blended margin, inventory requirements are also minimal, and there is basically no working capital risk. I see the service revenue as the “blade” portion of a “razor and blade” model, which is basically like a royalty stream on SIG’s installed base. Once a customer owns a SIG product and signs up for services, it doesn’t just bring in recurring revenue; it also provides SIG with multiple future touchpoints to monetize that relationship. At a high level, this dynamic improves overall earnings quality. A higher mix of service revenue should smooth out earnings volatility and reduce SIG dependence on running more promotions to bring customers through the door.

Valuation

I believe the path forward for growth to accelerate back to at least mid-single-digit percentages is getting more visible (the three brands mentioned above represent ~70% of total sales, and they are printing 6% CSG). Margin-wise, with SIG stepping up the value chain, services bringing in recurring customers without the need to spend on marketing, and lab-grown diamonds having structurally higher margins, I continue to stay confident margins can go back to 8%. The assumption I changed is the NTM P/E, up from 9x to 10x. The two main peers, Chow Tai Fook Jewellery Group and Watches of Switzerland Group, trade at 11x and 14x, respectively, with a mid-to-high single-digit revenue growth outlook, which is higher than SIG, but earnings-wise, all three players have a similar outlook (low teens). In that sense, I think SIG deserves to trade in the same range, but probably at a discount because of the slower revenue growth. At 10x, I think the stock could be worth $150.

Risk

Some brands are still dragging down overall performance, like James Allen and Blue Nile. If these banners cannot be stabilized, it would be tough for SIG to drive a strong earnings growth recovery. Furthermore, with credit, leasing, and BNPL now representing ~43% of sales, SIG exposure to the consumer credit cycle has gone up. If we go into a recession, there is a big risk that SIG could lose a sizable portion of these sales.

Conclusion

SIG is moving up the value chain, supported by stronger brands, structurally higher-margin lab-grown diamonds, and a growing services base that improves earnings quality. While not every banner is fixed, the parts that matter most are working and account for the majority of sales. I see room for multiples to expand higher as the market gains confidence in the earnings growth potential. I reiterate a buy rating on SIG.

data.title: Celestica: How It Escaped The Low-Margin Trap
data.description:
The Celestica Investment Thesis

Celestica Inc. (CLS) seems to be succeeding in escaping the low-margin factory business, and improving its business model as a result. This will make the company much more attractive in terms of its profitability. It is the beginning of a journey that, depending on the development of the design and software businesses, could eventually lead to long-lasting competitive advantages.

Since Celestica is also undervalued, I'm keeping my buy rating.

What was my previous coverage like

In my last article, I titled Celestica a 'follow the money' play, assuming that they would beat the analyst estimates. A little later, Celestica managed to achieve this with a double beat. I also considered the company to be undervalued, as I believed the market was underestimating its growth potential. But also because Celestica was cheaply valued compared to its peers. At the time, however, I was not sure whether Celestica had a long-term competitive advantage, which is why I initially only considered the shares to be attractive in the short term.

After taking a second look at the company, I can definitely see advantages over the previous competition. In my opinion, the company has evolved, which is why I want to write a new article.

The low-margin factory business

Within the peer group consisting of Jabil (JBL), Flex (FLEX), Sanmina (SANM), and Foxconn (FXCOF), Celestica stands out extremely positively. The company has the best gross profit margins as well as the best profit margins. However, that was not always the case. Until 2023, Celestica was caught in the same low-margin factory business as its peers. The margins in the assembler sector are simply incredibly low.

Celestica has, however, managed to achieve a ROIC of 25% in a previously unattractive sector, while most of the competition is experiencing a decline in capital efficiency, as can be seen in the above graph.

How did Celestica accomplish this?

Celestica's competitive advantages result from the company's change of business model from a purely hardware-based model to a hardware + design + software model. Celestica now designs its own switches. This gives them premium margins compared to the factory-only players. If we go to the Open Compute website and search for Celestica and its 800Gbe Data Center Switch, we see that Celestica's switch design specification is listed and Celestica is listed as the provider. However, when searching for competitors, one often receives results with contributions from other companies or no results at all.

The second point is that Celestica is leveraging the SONiC ecosystem. Since many will initially wonder what SONiC is, I will show the explanation from the Foundation's website.

Software for Open Networking in the Cloud (SONiC) is an open source network operating system (NOS) based on Linux that runs on switches from multiple vendors and ASICs. SONiC offers a full suite of network functionality, like BGP and RDMA, that has been production-hardened in the data centers of some of the largest cloud service providers.

Source: SONiC Foundation

SONiC is an open-source operating system used by companies such as Celestica, Broadcom (AVGO), and Marvell (MRVL). Since companies in the data center sector do not want to be trapped in ecosystems, they are very happy that there is open-source software available. With Celestica Solutions for SONiC, Celestica goes the extra mile by offering software support for its hardware, providing customers with a one-stop shop. Other low-margin hardware providers do not offer this service, or only in a very limited capacity.

This support allows Celestica to stand out from the competition. As a result, the company can charge a small premium, which later translates into a better margin.

Celestica Solutions for SONiC is supported on Celestica's entire portfolio of disaggregated networking switches, which includes multiple silicon options and various port and speed configurations, enabling production-ready deployment in a multitude of use cases from the data center to the edge.

Source: Celestica (PDF Document)

In other words, Celestica is no longer in the same league as assemblers, but is trying to become one of the companies that generates high margins through design and software.

I'm not claiming that Celestica will achieve similar gross profit and profit margins to Arista Networks (ANET) or Marvell (MRVL) in the next 24 months. However, I think it's clear that there's still enormous margin upside potential. Depending on the product mix and how things go with the 1.6 TB switches, I could see Celestica doubling its margins in the long term.

Celestica's Valuation

The fact that Celestica has outgrown its old peer group is also evident in the development of the multiple. In other words, the market currently believes that the company is more valuable.

However, even compared with Arista and Marvell, Celestica has a high multiple, which is why I don't see any multiple upside potential for Celestica at the moment. To be honest, I would say that Arista deserves a higher multiplier than Celestica, as Arista is currently in a different league when it comes to the business metrics.

In my opinion, the PEG ratio is the most suitable valuation method for Celestica. I consider anything under 1 or around 1 to be undervalued for high-quality companies. So, with a PEG ratio of 0.49, Celestica is significantly undervalued. At the time of my last article, the PEG ratio was at 0.9x, so the shares have become more undervalued despite the fact that the share price has risen by almost 30% since then.

Risks

The obvious risks are that we still don't know when the cycle will turn and when the production of the 1.6 T generation will begin exactly. It is also unclear whether Celestica will take the lead again there. Additionally, I see that Broadcom and the suppliers of the retimers and transceivers have a strong position in the supply chain, which could potentially lead to problems in the future. So, there is probably a supply chain risk.

Should the advanced technology solutions segment not return to growth, I also see an increased risk. This would mean that the proportion of the cyclical Connectivity and Cloud Solutions business would continue to increase even faster.

Conclusion

In summary, Celestica has succeeded in setting itself apart from the old competition, the factory-only providers. So the company has developed into a higher-level provider. I think the margins will continue to grow over the next few quarters, as I expect Celestica to focus more and more on design and software to avoid getting caught up in a price war. In a price war based solely on hardware, the low-cost providers would have an advantage.

data.title: AFK: Seeking Exposure To Africa Makes Sense, But This ETF Is Very Poorly Targeted
data.description:
Investment Thesis

VanEck Africa Index ETF (AFK) aims to provide some exposure to the broad African continent, and is the only ETF currently available for this purpose.

While I tend to agree with the investment case for owning African equities, the serious limitations of this specific fund lead to my sell rating, in spite of very strong performance over the last year. These limitations include inappropriate holdings resulting from passive selection rules, excessive concentration in the basic material and financial sectors, minimal exposure to African consumers, and a very high expense ratio.

Recent Performance

Looking at the graph above, it's easy to understand why investors with a short-term horizon might be attracted to AFK, with the ~78% total return almost 3.5x greater than would have been achieved by owning the S&P 500 over the past year.

However, considering this short-term growth within the context of longer term performance, the overall picture is a lot less convincing. AFK has returned just 6% versus the S&P's 667% (with dividends reinvested) since its inception in July 2008. Given the long-term mean-reverting tendency of markets, I think this second graph deserves more emphasis than the first, particularly from the point of view of a prudent, long-term oriented owner.

Bullish On Africa

While the long-term performance of AFK has been disappointing, this doesn't mean I am negative about the overall prospects for the continent.

Demographic considerations alone are a potent tailwind that should buoy the continent's equity markets. With a population approaching 1.6 billion and a median age of around 19 (versus 39 for the United States), Africa has a very young workforce and a lot of latent productivity. It's important to note that the continent's population is expected by the United Nations to reach ~2.5 billion by 2050 and approach 4 billion by 2100, providing a large base of consumers to be served by African companies.

In addition, it's worth keeping in mind that the efficiency and growth offered by the internet (and to an increasing extent A.I.) have had a much less significant effect on Africa broadly versus developed economies, so far. While in Sub-Saharan Africa internet usage has more than tripled since 2013, the 34% of the population currently using the internet is still extremely low compared with the United States, where 93% are internet users.

Africa also has enormous wealth in natural resources. Data from the U.S. Geological Survey shows that African countries have leadership positions in a number of critical minerals. South Africa produces 71% of the world's supply of platinum, while Guinea produces 29% of the world's bauxite (for aluminium) and Congo produces 76% of global cobalt. And these are just a selection of illustrative examples.

Structural Weakness

While demographic considerations and natural resource wealth are massive tailwinds for Africa, it's very clear to me that this doesn't translate into an automatic "buy" rating for AFK, since some scrutiny of what it owns is clearly warranted.

AFK tracks the MVIS® GDP Africa Index, which buys a selection of companies incorporated in Africa or generating at least 50% of revenues from the region. While the index attempts to weight holdings towards the relative sizes of each African country's economy (via GDP), this does not work very well in practice. Firstly, this is because many African countries do not have well developed equity markets (often in terms of liquidity), so they cannot be included at all. As shown below, only a small fraction (around eight) of the 54 countries in Africa are meaningfully represented in the fund (at least from the perspective of country of incorporation).

The GDP component to the index also proves somewhat contradictory to the "at least 50% of revenues" rule, since this results in selecting a large number of multinational mining companies headquartered in Western countries and with operations around the world, diluting the "pure play" African exposure.

As shown above, the ETF is very concentrated in mining stocks (basic materials) and financials (i.e. banks). These two categories account for a massive 72% of the ETF overall, posing some concentration risk.

From my point of view, this is somewhat disappointing, as when investing in an emerging market, it is often attractive to be exposed to the consumer side of the economy, which can benefit from a growing middle class. Unfortunately, AFK only has a 7.96% allocation to consumer cyclicals and a 1.46% allocation towards consumer defensives. This means investors are not getting any meaningful exposure to Buffett-style compounder type stocks (i.e. the Coca-Cola equivalents of Africa), and the lack of any technology exposure also limits economic exposure to the secular trend of an increasingly digital Africa, which I briefly highlighted earlier.

Another notable structural weakness is the "incorporated in Africa" rule. This results in some very strange holdings. For example, Royal Caribbean Cruises (RCL) represents 0.67% of the fund. Indian travel booking firm MakeMyTrip (MMYT) also appears, accounting for 1.44%. While neither of these companies have any material operations in Africa, the fact that they are (for tax reasons) incorporated in Liberia and Mauritius, respectively, means that they technically qualify for inclusion in the fund. This further demonstrates that the flawed selection rules result in a dilution of the pure-play Africa angle of the fund.

Fees

In addition to the poor targeting outlined, another big weakness of the fund is that its fee burden is very high and the bid/ask spread is wide, creating some friction for prospective owners.

AFK's 0.88% expense ratio is about 76% higher than the median across all ETFs and over time has the potential to significantly eat into total returns. After ten years, this level of fee results in an ~8.5% loss of total return, and after twenty years, the figure is ~16.2%. Investors may consider that owning individual African equities is a better choice, or go for specific country ETFs such as iShares MSCI South Africa (EZA), which has a slightly better 0.59% expense ratio. Notably, EZA offers a ~5.82% dividend yield, which is much more impressive than the just under 1% yield offered by AFK.

Upside Risk

Since I have already discussed the negative aspects of the fund, it is worth highlighting some paths that might result in continued outperformance for AFK, to provide some balance.

One reason that AFK might do well, at least in the short-term is the potential for continued outperformance for the mining sector broadly. As shown above, AFK is highly correlated with mining ETFs such as iShares MSCI Global Metals & Mining Producers (PICK). Since nearly 40% of AFK is represented by mining firms, this could support the fund in the short term. However, mining tends to be a cyclical industry, making this a less relevant consideration for long-term oriented Africa bulls.

A longer term development that could cause me to adjust my thesis would be the potential for equity markets across the continent to become more mature, resulting in greater exposure to African consumer-oriented firms and technology companies. Since this is already possible with markets further along in their development journey, such as China and India, this is not out of the question over the coming decades. I suspect that many prospective owners of AFK want exposure to the African "Coca-Cola" equivalent firms, and currently, this is affirmatively not what AFK offers. Were this to occur, it would make AFK a differentiated and valuable product in a way which it is currently not.

Conclusion

AFK is the only ETF product that claims to provide exposure across Africa. While I think this is a noble goal, in practice there are a number of reasons why this doesn't work well, including very rudimentary selection criteria and excessive concentration in banks and mining firms. Considering that investors are charged quite a lot for a rather poorly targeted strategy, I must award a sell rating to the fund.

data.title: GLDI: Upside Potential Is Capped
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

For investors seeking exposure to precious metals (gold), there are various alternatives on the stock market. These range from buying an index fund that replicates the price of an ounce of gold to speculating with financial derivatives such as options or futures. Today, we will analyze a hybrid vehicle known as ETRACS Gold Shares Covered Call ETN (GLDI). This works like an exchange-traded note (ETN), whose value depends on the performance of the NASDAQ Gold FLOWS 103 Index. Because it has an ETN structure, the investor accepts the credit risk of the issuer, UBS Group AG (UBS), which issues the exchange-traded note, which in turn is senior unsecured debt. This means that if UBS were to run into problems, investors could face a significant loss of capital, as the vehicle is not backed by physical assets, but rather by a promise of future payment (promissory note). As a first important point, this is not an investment vehicle for the general public, but rather for somewhat advanced investors.

With regard to the investment thesis, I recommend buying the vehicle without exceeding 10% of the investment portfolio, only if you are an investor familiar with financial option strategies and if the gold rally does not have parabolic momentum. Otherwise, it is better to buy the SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (GLD). Gold and its related products are heavily influenced by real interest rates, the strength of the dollar, and the inherent volatility of the gold market itself. As we will see later in the article, these variables are fairly aligned at the moment to continue the gold rally that began in 2023. If we add to this the fact that in 2026 there will be fiscal stimulus measures in almost all macro-economically relevant geographies (the US, Japan, the UK, and the European Union), this could lead to a more aggressive gold rally due to a partial increase in inflation. That is why I recommend buying GLD if this happens. As we will see below, the strategy followed by GLDI limits upward trend movements.

The Strategy

GLDI's strategy is based on selling call options on the GLD ETF. The index buys GLD as a long position and simultaneously sells call options on the same GLD to generate income (carry) through the collection of premiums for the sale of these options. This sale is carried out every month, with a monthly expiration and a strike price set at 103% of the GLD price (this is known as OTM - Out The Money options trading). The result of this strategy is that if the price of GLD rises, the ETN captures the monthly increase of 3% and collects the premiums for the sale of options. The problem with this is that if there is a sustained rally, such as the one that has been occurring and I believe will occur in the short to medium term, applying this strategy limits your profits to 3% per month plus the income from those premiums. As gold is a trending asset, I believe it is best to buy GLD directly.

ETN Overview

The image above shows a comparison between the cumulative returns of GLD vs. GLDI over the last year. As readers can see, we were right when we previously said that GLDI's strategy limits the benefits of direct exposure to gold. Another factor to consider is the level of fees associated with the vehicle analyzed. It is currently 0.65% per annum and accrues daily, reducing the indicative value of the ETN. Added to this are the notional transaction costs of the index for implementing the strategy, which in annualized terms is 0.84% of the notional value of the index itself.

Payoff Profile

Analyzing the vehicle's payment profile, we see that it achieves its objective of generating constant and growing income. This is not surprising if we look at the general trend of gold over the last few decades. We see that the dividend generated has grown by 62% in the last 12 months, providing a yield of almost 16% in the same period. If we compare this with gold, we see that the asset has grown by 70% in the same period.

Macro Drivers

Real Yields

By analyzing some macro drivers of gold, we can corroborate what we discussed in the first section of this analysis. In this first chart, we see real interest rates compared to the price of GLDI. When real rates fall, gold generally benefits because the opportunity cost of holding an asset that does not pay a coupon decreases. With various central banks cutting rates, and looking at the shape of the chart, I believe that real rates will gradually decline.

DXY - Dollar

The second important factor to monitor is the evolution of the dollar. When the dollar falls against a basket of currencies (DXY), this favors gold as it makes dollar-denominated investments more attractive to currencies other than the dollar itself. Today, we can see in the image that the dollar is moving within a small range, showing no signs of a reversal of the upward trend. This second factor leads me to believe that, as in 2025, 2026 could be a good year for the dollar. We will see how this comment stands the test of time.

The Tailwind

Finally, I have built a small indicator in TradingView, which shows the joint evolution of metrics such as real interest rates, the evolution of the dollar, and the financial conditions environment as measured by the NFCI indicator. Currently, the indicator shows that gold now has a moderate tailwind under these conditions (white line). The red line indicates the carry situation, relating the implied volatility and realized volatility of the market. At the moment, the market is paying expensive “insurance” against gold, which indicates that in the short term, GLDI may offer better coupons for the monthly sale of calls. With all this in mind, I recommend buying GLD. GLDI is only for experienced investors and should be used tactically. I suggest buying both, so you can monetize the entire rise in gold and at the same time have a recurring income via the sale of call options.

In 2026, I see GLDI as a potentially useful tactical instrument if gold progresses in an orderly fashion and the market continues to pay a good price for volatility (high IV/RV), because then the call overlap can transform a zero-coupon asset into an attractive monthly flow. However, if gold enters an aggressive rally phase, the opportunity cost of the capped upside increases, and I prefer GLD as the main exposure. Therefore, my approach is “core-satellite.” I use the GLD ETF as the core to capture the convexity of gold, and GLDI as a satellite (small size) when the macro and volatility checklist aligns. All this takes into account that, fiscally, GLDI payments are coupons and may be taxed as ordinary income for many investors.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Constellation Energy: Nuclear Expansion And Powerful Tailwinds Make This A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Constellation Energy (CEG) is a leading energy player focused on supporting the overall AI and data center sector. They have been making headlines for their revival of nuclear power center restarts and development to power the increasing demands of data centers for AI related tasks. Beyond their AI focus, they still have their lines of business focused on generating carbon-free energy for homes and businesses across the US. They are based out of Baltimore, Maryland, and their fleet powers over 20 million total end users.

CEG has a goal to eliminate 100% of their greenhouse gas emissions by 2040, which they plan to pair with their leading nuclear efforts to create a powerful player across multiple energy sources - all with a focus on clean energy.

We are bullish on CEG for its diversified business prospects and surging AI nuclear ambitions. The following section will review some of their highlights and macro trends that are relevant to the firm.

Highlights and relevant macro trends

Business remains focused on expansion of nuclear growth

Nuclear energy will become one of the most powerful and replicable ways to meet the enormous power demands of AI data centers, and CEG is well-positioned from a strategy perspective to address this need. Per their November 2025 earnings presentation, they share a slide that demonstrates their approach to meeting this demand through nuclear power.

It is clear that the firm’s leadership has outlined what they have to do and execute to become the leader in reviving nuclear power in the US, and with their targeted 2,250 MW of nuclear restarts on the roadmap, we believe that CEG should be able to win significant client contracts and deals in this space. We anticipate long-term deals, such as the one announced with Meta in the summer of 2025, will continue to roll in as CEG develops their nuclear capabilities further.

Powerful governmental support in the nuclear sector should be a tailwind for CEG

The Department of Energy has outlined a plan to add 35 GW of new capacity by 2035 and achieve a sustained pace of 15 GW per year by 2040 to help the country reduce carbon dioxide emissions and meet the demands of AI. With such monumental goals ahead, we are bullish on CEG as an established nuclear player and believe that they are in prime position to benefit from this governmental shift towards nuclear power. See this graph from the US Energy Information Administration that shows how US nuclear power is still behind its peak.

With so much room to grow, CEG is positioned to benefit strongly from this trend toward expanding nuclear power, both from the government and from the private sector.

The firm is actively growing through M&A

CEG announced this week the completion of their acquisition of Calpine Corporation, becoming the nation’s largest producer of electricity as a combined entity. Calpine is a leader in natural gas and geothermal electricity generation, which we view as beneficial, as this new business combination is now extremely diversified across geographies and energy types. This new company will have over 55 GW of total capacity, and the vision is for a holistic energy firm with advanced generation, storage, and distribution capabilities. The current administration has also voiced their support for increasing geothermal power output in the US, which shows that CEG is aiming to provide power across multiple critical categories of energy - and being involved in sectors the US government deems important is a fantastic place to be as a company. We believe in this diversified firm and their vision for the combined entity in scaling their business across multiple trending, critical segments.

Financial Trends

We look at how the company’s key financials have trended over time to understand their trajectory and stability.

Note: All of our charts will include a TTM bar because the data for the latest FY (2024) is over 1 year old. Keep in mind that there will be 1 quarter of overlap between the 2024 and TTM numbers.

Starting with revenue, we see that the company has posted relatively stable revenues over the last 4 years (plus the TTM). There is a bit of fluctuation that can be caused by changes in wholesale power prices, hedging activities, and capacity revenue.

For net income we see a slightly different picture, with 2021 and 2022 having posted losses. For 2021, we are seeing discrepancies between income statement numbers in Seeking Alpha vs the 10k vs a third source we checked (Yahoo Finance). As such, it is hard to nail down exactly why there was a net loss in 2021. Assuming that the 10k is the source of truth, we see that there were increased costs of purchasing power and fuel as well as a higher depreciation expense.

In 2022, while the depreciation expense did decrease, there was a further increase in purchased power and fuel expenses as well as an increase in operating and maintenance costs.

Looking at a wider range of years, we see that while the net losses in 2021 and 2022 seem to be isolated incidents, there is definitely a lot of variation in net income.

We look at the Net Margin as well to eliminate any fluctuation that could be caused by revenue changes and see a similar trend. This tells us that the operating costs can have a huge impact on earnings despite revenues.

Given the large variations in earnings, it is difficult to confidently calculate a valuation for CEG. However, looking at some of the trends we see—steadily increasing revenue, growth through M&A, and strong government investment—we are predicting an overall positive trend for the company's per share value.

Risks

Net Income Fluctuations

As we saw, the net income of the company tends to fluctuate a lot. This is mainly caused by changes in power/fuel costs (depreciation and operating/maintenance costs also play a part but not as large). From an investor perspective, this is a risk, as the company has little control over their power costs, which can fluctuate wildly and make the difference between a net gain and a net loss.

Nuclear energy carries operational and safety risks

There have been disasters in history where the view of nuclear energy has taken a blow - and CEG’s Three Mile Island plant was one of the sites of a major accident back in 1979 - and residents in the area have expressed their discomfort with the restarting of this plant to meet AI demand. While accidents are rare and improved security and safety regulations should decrease the risk of major crises, the risk is still existent and errors still may occur. Having an accident occur would be deeply detrimental to nuclear as a whole, and CEG would be significantly impacted.

Conclusion

CEG is a powerful, diversified energy player operating in some of the most critical areas of energy generation today. Their developments in nuclear and geothermal power are all backed with strong tailwinds from the US government, and they have major marquee clients such as Meta who have signed long-term deals with them.

We see steady and slightly growing top line revenue numbers indicating a solid core business. The fluctuations in net income caused by changes in energy costs could pose a risk to investors. However, with prudent management of hedging and power mixes, this impact could be minimized.

In all, we rate CEG as a buy.

data.title: Intel's U.S. Capex Boom Carries A Hidden Cyclical Risk (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
U.S. industrial policy is triggering one of the biggest domestic capex waves in decades, hinged on booming semiconductors and AI infrastructure. There's a visible boost to U.S. activity and GDP as tariffs, subsidies, and national-security priorities pull factory construction and equipment spending forward. Intel (INTC)-up 22.15% since my last analysis of the company-is at the center of this buildout. Its multiyear U.S. fab expansion is policy-accelerated investment at its finest. But remember, construction spending lifts near-term economic data, while the true test of shareholder value-and hence the sustainability of the current macro valuation regime-depends on what happens next, after the factories are built. Utilization needs to be high, pricing has to be firm, and fixed costs need to be absorbed by sustained output.

There's an unspoken, almost hidden, reality to this current boom. As construction fades and depreciation rises, returns will hinge on ROI. It's unknowable, but far less controllable than tariff-induced capex, which isn't as free-market supportive as it seems. When asset appreciation is pulled forward, whether through inflation or through structural repositioning, the almost inevitable consequence is correction.

Capex Optics vs. Returns Reality

The current U.S. capex cycle is in a boom phase right now. As of August 2025, manufacturing construction spending is at $220.4B, which is only slightly below the April 2025 peak of $227.6B. However, some of the most seasoned value investors will tell you that one man's boom is another man's bubble. The historically extreme level of industrial buildout driven by advanced tech and reshoring flatters near-term economic data, but it doesn't mean stock market returns will be durable. After all, construction spending boosts GDP mechanically while projects are underway, but it says little about the economics of the assets once operational.

There are also the bottleneck conditions that compound the macro valuation risk. U.S. power construction spending is currently at a $155.7B seasonally adjusted annual rate. This is clear evidence of grid, generation, and transmission bottlenecks gating both semiconductor fabs and AI data centers-in other words, there is not just natural supply-demand, re-shoring cyclicality at play, there is also infrastructure friction. This happened because tariffs changed boardroom incentives and acceptable IRRs-this is neither "good," nor "bad," but simply opens up the American economy to a different modus operandi-and it is cyclical ("one man's boom is another man's bubble").

If you don't fully understand the GDP mechanics at play, it would be worth doing so. GDP includes goods and services produced domestically, and factory construction counts during the build phase via investment spending. However, the factory asset itself stops contributing once the build is complete, whereby ongoing GDP related to the factory comes only from internal production. Such internal factory production is dependent on greater free-market principles than tariff-induced re-shoring: utilization and pricing. This boom is heavily based on one-time GDP factory build-out events and multi-year depreciation to follow-ladies and gentlemen, that is a capex bubble. It's not fully organic.

Intel: Policy-Capex Case Study

Intel is an excellent case study of an area of particular weakness in U.S. domestic industrial policy right now. Firstly, it is a large domestic manufacturer with extremely high fixed costs and long asset lives. Secondly, it is a direct recipient in CHIPS-linked funding structures. Intel has a long history of manufacturing abroad and continues to do so in several countries, but it has a head start as a key CHIPS partner. This allows us to examine its reshoring in light of present tariffs that are incentivizing other U.S. companies to manufacture domestically.

Intel's additions to property, plant, and equipment totaled $24.8B in 2022, $25.8B in 2023, and $23.9B in 2024. There were $1.9B of government incentives and $12.7B of partner capital contributions in 2024 alone. The company has already disclosed capital expenditure commitments of $14B in 2025 and $6B thereafter. However, the company has paused or canceled its planned projects in Germany and Poland, and it has also slowed construction in Ohio-all to align better with real demand.

Here's the kicker: Intel recorded $3.3B of impairment charges and accelerated depreciation in 2024, which was primarily the result of manufacturing capacity (heavily tied to Intel 7). The company also reported $50.4B of construction in progress at the same time, which leads to assets that will begin to depreciate as they enter service. If revenue growth does not offset the depreciation ramp, margins and operating income will come under pressure and further impairments are possible. In other words, remember the sell-off we saw in March 2025 related to tariff fears? Well, perhaps that was the prelude, because we have not yet had enough time to see the real effects. Whether the re-shoring is good geopolitically is another question to if it will lead to medium-term cyclicality and valuation pressure in the markets.

Intel is in a specifically vulnerable position. Intel Foundry generated $17.5B of revenue in 2024 with an operating loss of $13.4B. External demand isn't strong enough yet to de-risk the depreciation wave. That doesn't mean it never will, but it means we are entering a peak, and then a crest, and then a trough... whereby phase 1 of the AI buildout (heavily focused on U.S.-domestic capex) will culminate, followed by a rising demand wave as utilization is optimized-after a valuation reset.

Price Target

Intel looks expensive to me. Based on intrinsic value, we are not in a positive value re-rating environment for the stock anymore, but in a capex-induced bubble. It will likely de-rate in the next couple of years before the real utilization story unfolds more gradually.

I have drawn an entry band on the above chart, which is where I consider a reasonable, sentiment-derived but intrinsically-anchored "fair value." The margin of safety is higher than it would appear from pure sector relativity (forward P/E non-GAAP 420% higher than the sector median) and discounted cash flow, because the long-term structural upside from the AI/robotics supercycle is real, even if capex outcomes will not be consistently positively incremental.

To quantify this, my proposed entry prices are between $33 and $27.50 per share. At the current valuation, I expect consolidation given macro valuation limitations and the capex cyclicality dynamics discussed in sections 1 and 2 of this analysis. Within 24 months, I posit a likely retrace to $33 per share for Intel stock.

Conclusion: Hold

I am not bearish on Intel long-term, given that it is a company that is rather well-positioned in the autonomous-tech supercycle. That said, a value investor is trained to evaluate entry points, and this is actually a moment to tactically sell or long-term hold-perhaps to trim-INTC, but definitely not a moment to buy it. It is precisely in markets like these that valuations get ahead of themselves based on policy-derived narrative, but this is exactly the moment when you need to show restraint when others are committing irrational exuberance. Structural dynamics indicated by historical precedents make the probability of a correction following the Trump capex boom as being high. For that reason, I'm neutral on Intel stock for now.

Addendum: FY25 Earnings Preview

Intel is scheduled to report Q4 2025 earnings on January 22, providing a major checkpoint to assess policy and capex impacts. Management has been driving for about $18B in 2025 gross capex, but also slowing projects to align spending with demand-the rate of Intel's capex in 2026 will provide real evidence to the broader way re-shoring capex will unfold across other companies. Comments from management in the earnings call about gross margin sensitivity to impairment and GAAP depreciation will be critical to assess alongside construction-in-progress versus what's being placed into service-can the company profitably manage the depreciation ramp? I'd argue at this stage it's naïve to assume a straight line. If guidance implies a digestion year without a credible utilization ramp, then my Hold stance is more than justified.

data.title: CLOB: Not A Good Time To Own CLO Tranches (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We first started covering the VanEck AA-BB CLO ETF (CLOB) a year ago, when we praised the fund for bringing the structure and risk profile of a vertical slice of a CLO to market. However, we did not feel bullish in terms of forward price appreciation for the ETF, so we assigned it a 'Hold' rating. The fund is fairly flat from a price perspective, with a modest total return.

In today's article we are going to revisit CLOB, especially in light of the current macro developments that favor fixed rate assets over floating rate ones. We will show readers why we are a 'Sell' for this ETF into 2026.

The structure is good

What we still like about CLOB is its structure, which is a vertical slice of a CLO. This purely means that the name blends a number of CLO tranches in one fund:

  • AA: 42%
  • A: 23%
  • BBB: 21%
  • BB: 14%

The market already has specific tranche ETFs, such as the Janus Henderson B-BBB CLO ETF (JBBB) geared towards BBB CLOs, and the Eldridge BBB-B CLO ETF (CLOZ) targeting BB names. We have articles covering both those ETFs, and we have 'Sell' ratings associated with each one.

As a reminder, a CLO tranche looks as follows:

By virtue of building an ETF with various tranches, CLOB is like a vertical slice out of a CLO. Think about it as a cake, where a user slices a piece for consumption. CLOB is therefore more of a take on the entire asset class rather than a specific tranche. If we get a recession, BB names will underperform and possibly default, depending on the severity of such a recession. This means CLOB will suffer from a price perspective.

Similarly, in such a scenario, BBB tranches will also experience downward price pressure, but will not default given their subordination. The point is that an investor has no buffer here via CLOB, since they own pieces of each risk tranche as per the graph above.

Floating rate assets are coming under pressure

We are in a macro environment where rates are forecasted to come down significantly. The Fed is set to cut aggressively in 2026, and this will result in lower yields for CLOB. Currently the 30-day SEC yield is 6.2%, but it is set to move lower with Fed Funds and SOFR.

Remember that leveraged loans are set on SOFR, which in turn gets its levels from Fed Funds. Once Fed Funds move lower, SOFR follows, but with a lag (depending on tenor). Ultimately, SOFR correlates one-to-one with the Fed cuts.

The futures market currently gives us the following picture in terms of SOFR outlook:

The market is currently expecting a neutral rate close to 3%, which means another 70 bps lower in Fed Funds. One can extrapolate to a forward yield for the ETF of 5.5%, with an implied lag to Fed Funds.

In this macro environment, investors should not want to be in floating rate assets but fixed ones, especially for longer weighted average life holdings.

The fund is sensitive to credit spreads

Despite lacking a duration profile due to the floating-rate nature of the holdings, the fund does have credit sensitivity when it comes to spreads:

During the April 2025 market sell-off, we saw a -5% drawdown in the ETF, driven by spreads. In an actual recession this could go much lower, closer to -7% to -9%, depending on the severity of such a theoretical recession.

The issue currently facing the CLO asset class is two fold:

  1. SOFR is moving lower, thus lowering all-in yields
  2. Credit spreads are very tight historically

While we already outlined the forward for the rates market, let us take a look at some CLO spreads in historic context:

You can notice from the chart above that spreads (called discount margin in the chart above) are close to historic lows. This simply means that prices in turn are close to historic highs, and they are prone to downdrafts during sell-off events. You want to buy CLOs when spreads are wide and market participants shun the asset class, not when they are the most popular girl at the ball.

Some fund analytics

Let us also look at some of the fund's analytics here:

  • AUM: $0.15 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.45%
  • 30-day SEC Yield: 6.2%
  • Portfolio WAL: 1.8 years
  • Portfolio WA spread: 250 bps

The fund is indeed constructed fairly conservatively, with a low weighted average life of 1.8 years, and an acceptable expense ratio for the underlying asset class. Please remember the ETF is an active one; thus, risk metrics can change as the managers see fit for the underlying market conditions.

Conclusion

CLOB is an active ETF from VanEck, focused on a quasi-vertical slice of a CLO. The name is overweight AA and BBB tranches, and comes with an expense ratio of 0.45%. The fund will suffer as the Fed cuts, with the underlying asset class being floating rate. Furthermore, we find CLO spreads to be very tight, which means the next move during a risk-off event will be lower. We still like the fund structuring and what it does, but the macro environment is not attractive for this type of risk. We are a 'Sell' for CLOB in today's macro context.

data.title: Groupon: Focus On Billings Growth Recovery (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The S&P 500 has managed to hold near to record highs so far in 2026, but the mood in the markets appears to be anything but calm. With a vast array of potential downside macro catalysts against us (a shaky consumer economy, rising geopolitical tensions, a continued assault against Fed Chair Powell), investors are bracing for the next major leg downward.

My strategy to protect myself from downside remains the same: bias my portfolio exposure to more value-oriented stocks, particularly in the small and mid-cap space. Groupon (GRPN), in my view, is finally looking attractive after the stock took a recent dive despite strong results. The stock is still up ~40% over the past year, but down by more than half relative to mid-summer highs above $35. In my view, it's a great time for investors to reassess the bull case in this name again.

I last wrote a neutral article on Groupon in September, when the stock was trading at $23 per share. Since then, a combination of a ~30% share price decline plus continued strong billings performance have driven me to rethink my outlook on this stock. Given cheap valuation multiples and the opportunity for a stock market rotation out of large caps and into small, I'm upgrading Groupon to a buy.

Since an incredibly attractive valuation is the core reason to buy this stock, let's first start with a discussion of Groupon's latest valuation multiples. At current share prices near $16, Groupon trades at a market cap of $657.8 million. After we net off the $313.3 million of cash and investments on Groupon's latest balance sheet against $310.1 million of debt, the company's resulting enterprise value is $654.6 million.

For the remainder of FY25, Groupon has maintained its full-year billings guidance at 7-9% y/y growth, while raising full-year revenue expectations to $500-$505 million, or 2-3% y/y growth versus a wider $497-$505 million (1-3% range) previously. And while the company has moderated down its adjusted EBITDA expectations slightly (to $70-$72 million, from $70-$75 million previously), it is raising its FCF expectation to $60 million or a 12% midpoint margin, versus "at least $50 million" expected previously

For next year FY26, Wall Street is expecting revenue growth to more closely line up to billings growth, with consensus pointing to $556.5 million in revenue next year (+11% y/y growth). If we conservatively assume current-year adjusted EBITDA and FCF margins of 14.1% and 11.9% hold, respectively, adjusted EBITDA and FCF next year would be $78.5 million and $66.2 million.

This positions Groupon's valuation multiples at:

  • 9.2x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA and 8.3x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA
  • 10.9x EV/FY25 FCF and 9.9x EV/FY26 FCF

For a company that is expecting to return to ~10% revenue and billings growth with the additional cherry on top of consistent margin expansion, these are quite modest valuation multiples to pay. We add on top of this that Groupon is taking heavy advantage of its current share price slide by dedicating its ample cash resources toward buybacks. The company has waited to use up its years-old share repurchase authorization; it has $245 million left on this program, worth a whopping ~third of the company's current market cap. This buyback is also fully fundable from the company's existing balance sheet cash, not to mention ongoing growth in free cash flow.

Stay long here: Groupon is priced at a low enough level to enable a near-term rebound, especially with solid top-line performance underfoot.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Groupon's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Groupon's revenue grew 7% y/y to $122.8 million, slightly ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $122.0 million (+6% y/y).

The chart below, meanwhile, breaks out performance in the company's largest geographic segment of North America. As a reminder, billings represents actual customer purchases of local deals, while Groupon's revenue reflects when deals and offers are actually consumed. As such, billings is the most important forward-looking indictor of Groupon's growth trajectory.

North American billings grew 18% y/y to $294 million, continuing a three-quarter trend of re-acceleration to double-digit growth. Now, there were puts and takes in the quarter. Groupon notes that SMB (small and medium-sized businesses) continue to be the heart of the company's revenue base, and that revenue from SMB offers accelerated in Q3. That said, larger enterprise brands saw a slowdown in growth, which the company attributed to weakness in a number of enterprise campaigns.

That said, this isn't a cause yet for concern. Groupon continues to execute its hyper-local sales strategy, chasing supply in select attractive markets. For example, the company recently reallocated a lot of its sales headcount to Chicago, and in turn saw billings in the Chicago metro area grow at double the rate of the North America business (implying mid-30s growth). The company intends to keep driving a local-first sales approach going forward.

On the demand side of the house, the company notes that discovery stemming from AI search engines is growing. Per the company's commentary on the Q3 shareholder letter:

In Q2, we highlighted the early momentum we were seeing in AI-powered search traffic. This is a dynamic environment with frequent algorithm changes, but our platform is navigating these changes well, and we saw AI search traffic tracking toward all time highs in September. While this remains a small portion of our overall traffic today and did not have a significant impact on our Q3 financial results, the trajectory is encouraging. In recent months, we've increased our visibility to AI crawlers and large language model indexing through structured data optimization. We're building the technical infrastructure required for AI native commerce, including Modern API architecture, AI-ready search & relevance and AI-ready checkout."

Meanwhile, international also showed strong performance. Excluding the company's exit from Italy, the company's billings grew 15% y/y. Similar to the U.S., revenue growth also accelerated here to 8% y/y growth on a normalized basis.

Groupon also delivered $17.5 million in adjusted EBITDA, rising 18% y/y and hitting a 14.3% margin that improved 140bps versus 12.9% in the year-ago quarter.

Key takeaways

It's quite evident from recent performance that Groupon's strategy of selectively chasing supply in high-growth markets while emphasizing a localized sales force is working. Billings and revenue are all on an uptrend, providing tailwinds for adjusted EBITDA. We're more than happy to take a position in this stock at ~10x FCF.

data.title: Delek: Sum Of The Parts Points To Substantial Upside
data.description:
Shares of Delek US Holdings (DK) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 52%, as regulatory activities have greatly improved the company’s financial position. That said, shares have dropped about 25% from their recent highs, as focus has shifted from regulatory benefits to a weakening of the macro refining environment. Recent geopolitical actions from Venezuela to Iran have further increased intrigue around the sector. I last covered shares in November when I downgraded them from a “strong buy” to a “buy.” With the stock down 20% since then, I should have downgraded them even more significantly. Given recent developments and this underperformance, now is a good time to revisit Delek.

Refining spreads have narrowed

Looking first at the macro environment, the primary driver of the decline in Delek (and many of the refining stocks) is quite clear. As you can see below, crack spreads (which measure how much refiners make turning crude oil into products like gasoline and diesel) have plunged in recent weeks and are now in the low $20s, after having passed $30. While I was a bit surprised how wide spreads become given it was a quiet hurricane season, the magnitude of the retrenchment has been equally surprising. I view ~$25 as the long-term norm for refining margins, so we have swung from an environment that is clearly favorable to refiners to one that is currently unfavorable.

Crack spreads are driven by the relative availability of crude vs refined products, and we have seen a dramatic increase in gasoline inventory. As you can see below, a few months ago, gasoline inventories were at 5-year lows. While they typically rise during the winter as demand slows, the magnitude of this rise has been jarring, so much so that inventories are now at 5-year highs. This points to there being a fairly significant slowdown in demand, which has raised concerns about the product market being oversupplied and keeping spreads narrow.

Now, inventories and demand can be particularly volatile around the holiday season, and my view is that the US economy is likely to grow modestly (~2%) in 2026, which should support stable demand over the course of the year and keep product inventories from rising too much. Beyond this, we should see some support on the crude supply side, given the government change in Venezuela.

Venezuelan production has plunged over the past decade as underinvestment and sanctions have crippled its productive capacity. I do not expect production to return back to mid-2010 levels anytime soon, as that will likely require years of investment. That said, efforts to re-route previously sanctioned oil into the US to be refined (given our expertise at refining heavy oils) should increase the domestic supply of crude modestly, helping to put a floor under crack spreads. Over the medium term, more Venezuelan production adds to my view that $25 is a normalized level for crack spreads.

EPA action provides material benefit to Delek

Apart from this significant shift in the macro environment, Delek is a significant winner of regulatory actions. In the company’s third quarter reported back on November 7th, Delek reported an outsized $7.13, well ahead of estimates. However, this included a one-time gain from the favorable resolution of its petitions to the EPA for relief from renewable fuel standards under the small refinery exception (“SRE”). The SRE allows companies like Delek to be relieved of their RFS burden.

By mid-2026, Delek expects to receive $400 million in cash from this decision. As a reminder, this windfall represents payments from prior years 2019-2024. Delek received full approval of its 2023-2024 petitions, but only a partial approval for 2019-2022. The company plans to pursue additional compensation for those years. Beyond this, Delek expects 100% of its refining output with qualify from the SRE going forward, significantly improving ongoing cash flow. This will be a ~$200 million ongoing benefit to the company’s cash flow.

Management plans to use this cash inflow “prudently.” I read this to mean that investors should expect Delek to work down the excess cash position over time, rather than immediately. Additionally, some cash will be devoted to debt reduction and not just share buybacks; still, the company’s financial flexibility is greatly enhanced. Delek has $265 million of stand-alone net debt, and I expect DK to eventually target a ~neutral net debt posture.

DK has significant 2026 cash flow, enabling buybacks

On January 13th, Delek released a brief 2026 update. Most notably, it expects $200-200 million of cap-ex and turnaround spending. This includes a Q1 turnaround, which temporarily reduces throughput. Given the weaker crack spread environment, this is an opportune time for such work. Beyond that, the company has a new inventory intermediation agreement, which should unlock about $40 million of cash. Including its distributions from DKL, DK should generate at least $400 million of cash flow that can be distributed to investors in 2026, even in a ~$20 crack spread environment.

It is also worth noting that alongside Q3 earnings, Delek increased the expected benefits of cost-cutting program from $150 to $180 million. The combination of its cost-saving program and SRE relief have removed over $300 million from the company’s prior cost structure. This is already enabling capital returns. During Q3, Delek repurchased $15 million of stock in addition to its dividend. With proceeds from the SRE decision, I see scope to increase buyback activity. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 5% from a year ago, and DK has steadily reduced its share count over time. Shares offer a 3.5% dividend yield.

Valuation makes DK a strong buy

When valuing Delek, it is important to remember that the company owns a ~33.9 million share position in Delek Logistics Partners (DKL). This stake is worth just over $1.6 billion, and it also provides DK with about $150 million of annual cash flow from DKL’s distributions. This stake is worth over $26 per share, meaning that the rest of Delek is being valued at about $250 million at $30, alongside its net $265 million of cash. This entire $515 million enterprise value is covered by the 2026 DKL distribution of $150 million and $400 million of SRE cash flow from the EPA’s ruling. That value’s Delek’s refineries as essentially zero.

Frankly, I think investors are focused too much on near-term gasoline inventories and ignoring the bigger picture on a valuation basis. Even at $100 million of free cash flow per year (giving no credit for its cost saving program or DKL distributions), DK has a stand-alone equity value of ~$15/share at 10x free cash flow. Combined with its stake in DKL, that is a fair value of $40. I see fair value closer to $25, given my view that free cash flow is closer to $200 million ex-DKL and haircutting its cost-cutting program partially, or $50 all-in.

At $30, markets are ascribing no enterprise value to DK’s wholly owned businesses, factoring in 2026 cash inflows and its DKL stake. This has created a significant margin of safety, even in a weak refining market. With 25+% upside, I view DK as one of the most compelling value stocks in the market, and as such, I am upgrading shares back to a “strong buy” on their pullback, with 2026 buybacks likely to help push shares towards fair value.
